questions.question: What is an electronic communications network?
questions.expected_answer_summary: Transmission systems, including switching or routing equipment, enabling the conveyance of signals, regardless of the type of information conveyed.

questions.question: What is an electronic communications service?
questions.expected_answer_summary: A service normally provided for remuneration via electronic communications networks — including internet access, interpersonal communications services, and transmission services.

questions.question: What is a very high capacity network?
questions.expected_answer_summary: A network meeting BEREC criteria for down/uplink bandwidth, resilience, latency, with performance equivalent to optical fibre up to multi-dwelling buildings or base stations.

questions.question: What is a number-based interpersonal communications service?
questions.expected_answer_summary: An interpersonal communications service that connects with publicly assigned numbering resources from a national or international numbering plan.

questions.question: What does 'significant market power' mean?
questions.expected_answer_summary: An undertaking is deemed to have SMP if it enjoys a position equivalent to dominance under competition law — significant power, alone or jointly, to behave independently of competitors, customers, and consumers.

questions.question: What is the market analysis procedure?
questions.expected_answer_summary: National regulatory authorities define relevant markets, analyse effective competition, designate undertakings with SMP, and impose proportionate remedies. BEREC and Commission consultation applies.

questions.question: How are penalties applied under this Directive?
questions.expected_answer_summary: Member States set appropriate, effective, proportionate and dissuasive penalties for infringements, including fines and non-criminal periodic penalties. Art. 22(3)-specific penalties apply to misleading information.

questions.question: What is the procedure for consistent application of remedies across Member States?
questions.expected_answer_summary: Draft measures affecting trade between Member States must be notified to the Commission, BEREC, and other NRAs; the Commission may require amendment or withdrawal.

questions.question: What rules apply to the authorisation of electronic communications networks and services?
questions.expected_answer_summary: A general authorisation regime applies; specific conditions are limited to those listed in Annex I. Declarations replace individual licences for most providers.

questions.question: How is radio spectrum assigned?
questions.expected_answer_summary: General authorisation is the default; individual rights of use where justified. Member States apply objective, transparent, non-discriminatory, proportionate selection criteria.

questions.question: Which articles govern universal service obligations?
questions.expected_answer_summary: Title III of Part III (Articles 84-92) governs universal service availability, affordability, quality, designation of providers, and financing.

questions.question: What obligations apply to undertakings with significant market power?
questions.expected_answer_summary: NRAs may impose obligations on transparency, non-discrimination, accounting separation, access to and use of specific network facilities, price control, and cost accounting.

questions.question: Which articles concern end-user rights?
questions.expected_answer_summary: Part III Title II (Articles 99-116) covers end-user rights: non-discrimination, information requirements, contract durations, bundled offers, switching, emergency communications.

questions.question: Which articles concern cross-border electronic communications?
questions.expected_answer_summary: Article 65 covers transnational markets; Article 96 covers provision of cross-border services and the one-stop-shop procedure.

questions.question: How is BEREC involved in regulatory procedures?
questions.expected_answer_summary: BEREC provides opinions on draft measures, issues guidelines (including on VHCN), and coordinates consistent application of the Directive.

questions.question: Does this Directive cover broadcasting content?
questions.expected_answer_summary: No. The Directive applies to networks and services, not to broadcasting content — content regulation is governed by the Audiovisual Media Services Directive.

questions.question: What are the VAT rates for telecoms services?
questions.expected_answer_summary: The Directive does not set VAT rates. VAT is governed by other EU instruments, not EECC.

questions.question: Does this Directive apply to financial services?
questions.expected_answer_summary: No. The Directive is the European Electronic Communications Code and does not regulate financial services.

questions.question: Does the Directive set consumer data privacy rules?
questions.expected_answer_summary: Privacy of electronic communications is governed by the ePrivacy framework, not by EECC. EECC focuses on market access, spectrum, end-user rights.

questions.question: What obligations does an SMP undertaking face regarding access to civil engineering?
questions.expected_answer_summary: An NRA may impose obligations to meet reasonable access requests to civil engineering (ducts, poles, cabinets) where replication would be economically inefficient or physically impracticable.

questions.question: What process determines whether co-investment commitments exempt an SMP undertaking from access obligations?
questions.expected_answer_summary: Under Art. 76 an SMP undertaking may offer co-investment commitments; Art. 79 sets the commitment procedure with NRA market-testing and binding decisions.

questions.question: When can national regulators refrain from imposing ex ante remedies?
questions.expected_answer_summary: NRAs refrain when the market is effectively competitive (Art. 67), when qualifying co-investment offers exist (Art. 76), or for wholesale-only operators meeting Art. 80 conditions.

questions.question: What safeguards apply to emergency communications?
questions.expected_answer_summary: Art. 109 requires free-of-charge access to emergency services via 112; Art. 110 mandates public warning systems for imminent or developing major emergencies and disasters.

questions.question: Under what conditions may Member States impose must-carry obligations?
questions.expected_answer_summary: Member States may impose reasonable must-carry obligations on networks used for distribution of radio or TV broadcast channels, where a significant number of end-users use the network as main means of reception, and only for specified general-interest objectives.

questions.question: What is the Body of European Regulators for Electronic Communications?
questions.expected_answer_summary: BEREC is established by the BEREC Regulation and contributes to consistent application of the regulatory framework and the development of the internal market.

questions.question: What is a 'national regulatory authority'?
questions.expected_answer_summary: A body entrusted by a Member State with any regulatory tasks assigned by this Directive or BEREC Regulation.

questions.question: How are disputes between undertakings resolved?
questions.expected_answer_summary: NRAs issue binding decisions on disputes between providers within four months (Art. 26); cross-border disputes follow Art. 27 coordination procedures.

questions.question: What procedure applies to changes in contractual conditions for end-users?
questions.expected_answer_summary: End-users must be notified of any change in contractual conditions at least one month in advance and given the right to terminate without penalty.

questions.question: Which provisions concern number portability?
questions.expected_answer_summary: Art. 106 requires Member States to ensure number portability for subscribers to number-based interpersonal communications services, on request, with specific timeframes.

questions.question: Which articles cover security of networks and services?
questions.expected_answer_summary: Art. 40 mandates appropriate security measures by providers; Art. 41 provides for competent authorities' implementation, auditing, and notification of significant incidents.
